proposals.rationale: Committee dispersion is moderate (0.67 agreement) with Bull and Skeptic converging on 'crowded' and Bear holding 'high' — the majority read lands at 'crowded', which is the existing call and therefore requires no change under burden-of-proof rules. All three seats independently note ETN (+23% YTD, ~10-29% above 200-DMA) and GEV (+43% YTD, ~29% above 200-DMA) are extended. The shortage narrative (128-144 week lead times, 30% fleet shortfall, pv magazine 2026-05-11, POWER Magazine 2026-01-02) is explicitly consensus (non_consensus: false) — it is widely cited, not divergent. No seat produced a d
proposals.variant_view: Consensus correctly identifies the physical shortage but misses the duration asymmetry: the real alpha is not in the next 12-18 months of scarcity (already priced) but in whether OEM margin structures lock in pricing power through 2028-29 via long-dated service contracts and tariff-insulated domestic content requirements. If ETN or GEV disclose multi-year, fixed-price transformer supply agreements with utility or hyperscaler counterparties in upcoming 10-Qs, that converts a 'crowded narrative trade' into a 'compounding earnings durability' story — a qualitatively different re-rating catalyst t
proposals.bear_case: The steelmanned bear is straightforward and deserves full weight: at these valuations, the shortage does not need to be wrong — it only needs to be 'less bad than feared' to trigger de-rating. Three mechanisms converge by 2027: (1) Extreme lead times (4 years) are themselves the strongest capex incentive signal; South Korean, Indian, and reshored domestic capacity responses are already underway and will hit delivery windows precisely in 2027-29, the consensus 'bind' period. (2) AI load-management and grid-edge bypass technology (ENTO/GRDX, 2025-12-04) reduces effective transformer demand per M
